title: My Dog Ate a Marijuana Brownie: Emergency Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ingested a marijuana brownie, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about the risks of THC and chocolate toxicity for dogs.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your dog eats a marijuana brownie, it is a high-priority medical emergency due to the combined toxicity of THC and chocolate. Contact your veterinarian immediately to manage potential cardiac, neurological, and respiratory complications.

What happens if my dog eats a marijuana brownie?

Ingesting a marijuana brownie means your dog has consumed two potentially lethal toxins: THC (tetrahydrocannabinol) and chocolate. While THC affects the central nervous system, chocolate contains theobromine and caffeine, which can cause severe cardiac and neurological distress. This combination is particularly dangerous because the fat content in brownies can also trigger pancreatitis.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog eats a marijuana brownie?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a medical emergency; contact your veterinarian or the nearest pet emergency clinic immediately.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear before seeking help.
  • Chocolate ingestion can cause rapid heart rate and seizures.
  • THC toxicity can lead to extreme lethargy, urinary incontinence, and respiratory depression.
  • Prompt professional intervention is the most effective way to ensure your dog's safety.

Why should I take a photo of the brownie packaging for the veterinarian?

  • Take a photo of the brownie packaging or the remaining portion before heading to the vet.
  • The ingredient list helps the veterinary team determine the concentration of THC and the type of chocolate involved (dark chocolate is much more toxic than milk chocolate).
  • A photo allows the vet to check for other hidden toxins like Xylitol, a common sugar substitute that is deadly to dogs.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following marijuana ingestion in dogs, clinical signs typically manifest within 30-90 minutes and can persist for up to 72 hours. Common clinical signs include depression, ataxia, bradycardia, hypothermia, vocalization, hypersalivation, vomiting, diarrhea, urinary incontinence, seizures, and coma. Diagnosis is based on a history of exposure and typical clinical signs, although confirmation can be aided by urine testing at human hospitals or with over-the-counter drug test kits early in the course of exposure. Treatment consists of supportive care, including inducing emesis and administering activated charcoal if the exposure is recent and there are no contraindications. Comatose animals require monitoring for aspiration pneumonia, IV fluids, treatment for hypothermia, and frequent rotation to prevent dependent edema or decubital ulceration. Diazepam can be administered for sedation or to control seizures. Treatment and monitoring should continue until all clinical signs have resolved.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3041)

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of THC toxicity in dogs?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include stumbling or lack of coordination (ataxia), dilated pupils, sensitivity to touch or sound, slow heart rate, and leaking urine.

faqs.question: How long does it take for a dog to show signs of poisoning?
faqs.answer: Signs typically begin to show between 30 minutes and 3 hours after ingestion, though the effects of the chocolate may manifest sooner than the THC.

faqs.question: Can I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Never induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian. If the dog is already showing neurological signs, inducing vomiting can lead to aspiration pneumonia.
